title: How (and why) to take a logarithm of an image
summary: 3Blue1Brown presents a video exploring how logarithmic transformations can be applied to images, using Escher's Print Gallery as a case study. The video demonstrates connections between complex analysis, conformal maps, and visual art through animated explanations and mathematical rigor.
writeup: This video unpacks the mathematics behind M.C. Escher's 1956 lithograph 'Print Gallery,' following the 2003 analysis by mathematicians De Smit and Lenstra. The central problem is how Escher converted a self-similar Droste-effect image (where the picture contains itself at 1/256th scale) into a smooth circular loop where zooming in is encoded as a continuous gaze wandering around the canvas. The mechanism is a warped grid acting as a mesh warp, and the open question of where this grid comes from points toward the complex logarithm as its geometric origin, which is the mathematical through-line the video builds toward.
key_points: Escher's construction decomposes into three steps: start with a straight self-similar Droste image where the nested copy is 256 times smaller than the original; construct a warped grid that encodes a factor-of-4 scaling between each adjacent corner; then use that grid as a mesh warp to copy the source image piece by piece into the final circular loop.

The Droste effect is the graphic design term for a self-similar image nested inside itself; Escher's version uses a far deeper zoom (factor of 256) than the early-20th-century marketing examples that popularized the name, and a simplified pedagogical version with a factor-of-16 zoom distributes a 2x scale change across each of the four corners of the square.

A mesh warp is a standard graphic design technique: overlay a regular grid on the source image and a warped grid on the destination canvas, then copy each tiny source square to its corresponding destination square; at that small scale each patch is locally undistorted, making the copying mechanically straightforward even though the global result is highly curved.

Escher deliberately chose 'serial types of objects' such as rows of prints along a wall or blocks of houses in a town at each distinct scale, because without repeating cyclic elements at every zoom level the self-contained loop would be perceptually incoherent to a viewer.

De Smit and Lenstra reverse-engineered the unwarped Droste source image from the original lithograph with help from Dutch artists Hans Richter and Jacqueline Hofstra; the method they used to extract it amounts, in a precise sense, to taking the logarithm of the original piece, a connection the transcript promises to develop fully later in the video.

A diffusion model given the task of filling in the blank central circle of the Print Gallery fails entirely, because that circle compresses genuine geometric ambiguity: approaching it from any direction assigns it a different role (village buildings, picture frame, gallery interior), and no single set of pixels resolves all three simultaneously.

Distributing the Zoom Across Four Corners
